issues.original: For most of the 2010s, saying 'deep learning for time series' meant using LSTM. The story changed in 2018 when Bai, Kolter, and Koltun published *An Empirical Evaluation of Generic Convolutional and Recurrent Networks for Sequence Modeling*. Their result was surprisingly simple: use a stack of 1-D convolutions, make them causal (no peeking at the future), space the filter taps exponentially (dilation), wrap the whole thing in residual connections, and train. Task after task, the resulting **Temporal Convolutional Network** (TCN) matched or beat LSTM/GRU — while training several times faster because every time step in the forward pass runs in parallel.
issues.fixed: For most of the 2010s, 'deep learning for time series' meant using LSTM. The story changed in 2018 when Bai, Kolter, and Koltun published *An Empirical Evaluation of Generic Convolutional and Recurrent Networks for Sequence Modeling*. Their result was surprisingly simple: use a stack of 1-D convolutions, make them causal (no peeking at the future), space the filter taps exponentially (dilation), wrap the whole thing in residual connections, and train. Task after task, the resulting **Temporal Convolutional Network** (TCN) matched or beat LSTM/GRU — while training several times faster because every time step in the forward pass runs in parallel.

issues.original: This chapter unpacks why that recipe works. We will derive the receptive-field formula that makes dilation worth caring about, walk through the residual block step by step, and finish with two production-grade case studies (traffic flow and multivariate sensor forecasting) using a PyTorch implementation you can copy out.
issues.fixed: This chapter explains why that recipe works. We'll derive the receptive-field formula that makes dilation important, walk through the residual block step by step, and finish with two production-grade case studies (traffic flow and multivariate sensor forecasting) using a PyTorch implementation you can copy out.

issues.original: Before TCN, the deep-learning playbook for time series involved stacking two LSTM layers, adding attention if desired, and training for a long time. It worked, but every part of the pipeline had issues:
issues.fixed: Before TCN, the deep-learning playbook for time series involved stacking two LSTM layers, adding attention if desired, and training for a long time. It worked, but every part of the pipeline had issues:

issues.original: - **Sequential forward pass.** To compute hidden state [FORMULA] you need [FORMULA]. The GPU sits idle waiting for the previous step. Doubling the sequence length doubles wall-clock time even with infinite parallel hardware.
issues.fixed: - **Sequential forward pass.** To compute the hidden state [FORMULA], you need [FORMULA]. The GPU sits idle waiting for the previous step. Doubling the sequence length doubles the wall-clock time, even with infinite parallel hardware.

issues.original: - **Vanishing/exploding gradients through time.** Backprop has to traverse [FORMULA] multiplicative steps. LSTMs help via gates, but anything past ~200 steps becomes fragile. People use gradient clipping, layer norm, and careful initialization to keep training stable.
issues.fixed: - **Vanishing/exploding gradients through time.** Backpropagation must traverse [FORMULA] multiplicative steps. LSTMs help via gates, but anything past ~200 steps becomes fragile. People use gradient clipping, layer norm, and careful initialization to keep training stable.

issues.original: - **Hidden-state opacity.** 'Why did the model predict this?' usually has no good answer because the hidden state mixes everything.
issues.fixed: - **Hidden-state opacity.** 'Why did the model predict this?' usually has no good answer because the hidden state mixes everything.

issues.original: - **Hyperparameter tax.** The number of layers, hidden dimensions, gate variants, dropout types, and recurrent dropout positions all interact. A bad combination can waste a day of training before you notice.
issues.fixed: - **Hyperparameter tax.** The number of layers, hidden dimensions, gate variants, dropout types, and recurrent dropout positions all interact. A bad combination can waste a day of training before you notice.
